did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a machine learning approach, specifically transfer learning with an EfficientNet-B1 deep learning model, to classify dermatoscopic skin cancer images from the HAM10000 dataset into seven distinct skin lesion classes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in PLOS ONE. It presents original experimental work involving the design and validation of a new machine learning-based diagnostic tool using the HAM10000 dataset, including specific methods (transfer learning with EfficientNet-B1), results (accuracy of 84.3%, F1 scores), and statistical analysis.

title: Design and validation of a new machine-learning-based diagnostic tool for the differentiation of dermatoscopic skin cancer images
brief_summary: This study developed and validated a machine learning-based diagnostic tool to classify dermatoscopic skin cancer images into seven distinct lesion classes using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers employed transfer learning with an EfficientNet-B1 deep learning model, incorporating image pre-processing and data augmentation techniques. The model was evaluated on its ability to differentiate between melanocytic nevi, melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and other pigmented skin lesions. The primary result was an overall classification accuracy of 84.3% on the test set. Specifically, the model achieved an F1 score of 0.93 for melanocytic nevi, while scores for other classes ranged from 0.54 to 0.80. The authors conclude that this approach offers a promising method to assist dermatologists in improving the diagnosis of pigmented skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified in the HAM10000 dataset; N=327 in dataset, N=327 in Table 1; F1 score 0.63.
Classified in the HAM10000 dataset; N=514 in dataset, N=514 in Table 1; F1 score 0.72.
Classified in the HAM10000 dataset; includes seborrheic keratoses, lichen-planus-like keratoses, and solar lentigo; N=1099 in dataset, N=1089 in Table 1; F1 score 0.70.
Classified in the HAM10000 dataset; N=115 in dataset, N=115 in Table 1; F1 score 0.54.
Classified in the HAM10000 dataset; N=1113 in dataset, N=1111 in Table 1; F1 score 0.58.

Classified in the HAM10000 dataset; includes cherry angiomas, angiokeratomas, and pyogenic granulomas; N=142 in dataset, N=142 in Table 1; F1 score 0.80.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion classes (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermatoscopic skin cancer images using an EfficientNet-B1 model with a softmax layer with 7 nodes. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) from the HAM10000 dataset. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (84.3%) and F1 scores for each class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, collected over 20 years from two different sites: the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna, Austria, and the skin cancer practice of Cliff Rosendahl in Queensland, Australia. Data records are deposited at the Harvard Dataverse and accessible via the ISIC archive.

use.training: The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset using transfer learning with an EfficientNet-B1 architecture. Data augmentation techniques were applied to increase instances.
use.testing: The model was evaluated on the test set of the HAM10000 dataset, achieving an overall accuracy of 84.3%.
use.other: Used to develop a web application for classifying skin cancer lesions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize the dataset’s characteristics, presented as frequencies, percentages for categorical variables, mean ± standard deviation, and median (min-max) for continuous variables.
To compare means of continuous variables.
To compare mean age between different classes of skin cancer and between different classes of sex.
To perform post hoc analyses following the ANOVA test to determine specific differences between groups.
To determine statistical significance for all reported tests.

hypothesis_testing.test: One-way ANOVA, Scheffé post hoc criterion, Chi-square test, Independent-sample T-test, Pearson correlation coefficients, Linear regression
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in the mean age between different classes of skin cancer; there is no statistically significant difference in the mean age between different classes of sex.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in the mean age between different classes of skin cancer; there is a statistically significant difference in the mean age between different classes of sex.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A P-value of less than 0.05 was considered to be statistically significant. The ANOVA test demonstrated a statistically significant main effect of skin cancer type on age (F [6, 9951] = 470.117, p < .001) and a significant effect of gender on age (F [2,9955] = 8.76, p < 0.001). Post hoc analyses indicated that the average age was significantly lower in the melanocytic nevi class than in all other 6 skin cancer classes with p values less than 0.001.

HAM10000.notes: The model achieved an accuracy of 84.30% on the unseen test set with a loss of 0.4387. The model performed best in detecting melanocytic nevi lesions.

HAM10000_Class_Performance.notes: Performance differences between classes were attributed to the highly imbalanced nature of the dataset. The model's worst predictions were analyzed by sorting wrong predictions by inferred probability.

Training_Epochs.notes: The model was trained for 19 epochs. Learning rate started at 1.0e-03, reduced to 1.0e-04 after epoch 12, then to 1.0e-05, 1.0e-06, and finally 1.0e-08. Final validation accuracy was 84.30%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet (implied by 'transfer learning' and 'existing pre-trained models'), with top layer deleted and replaced.

architecture_details.backbone: EfficientNet-B1 (variant of baseline EfficientNet-B0 created through compound scaling)
architecture_details.modifications: Top layer deleted; Global Average Pooling 2D layer added; Softmax layer with 7 nodes added on top
architecture_details.fine_tuning: Top 50 layers of EfficientNet-B1 unfrozen for fine-tuning after initial feature extraction phase

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Started at 0.001; reduced to 0.0001 after 12 epochs; reduced to 1.0e-5, 1.0e-6, and 1.0e-8 in subsequent epochs using ReduceLROnPlateau callback
training_details.epochs: 19 total (15 for feature extraction, 4 for fine-tuning)
training_details.data_augmentation: Random width shift (-20% to +20%), random height shift (-20% to +20%), random max 0.2-degree shear angle, random horizontal and vertical flip, nearest pixel fill for empty pixels

classification_or_output_head.activation: Softmax (normalizes output to probability distribution over 7 predicted classes)

preprocessing_techniques: Labelling images using dataset metadata and transferring them into respective folders
Resizing images to (240, 240, 3) tensors using bilinear interpolation resampling

Data augmentation including random width shift (-20% to +20%), random height shift (-20% to +20%), random max 0.2-degree shear angle, random horizontal and vertical flip, and filling empty pixels with nearest pixel

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Shifted by -20% to +20% of image width.
Shifted by -20% to +20% of image height.
Random max 0.2-degree shear angle in a counter-clockwise direction.
Empty pixels were filled by the nearest pixel.

conclusions: The study successfully classified seven distinct skin lesions in the HAM10000 dataset using an EfficientNet-B1 model, achieving an overall accuracy of 84.3%.
The model performed best in detecting melanocytic nevi lesions with an F1 score of 0.93.
F1 scores for other classes were: Vascular lesions (0.80), Basal Cell Carcinoma (0.72), Benign Keratosis (0.70), Actinic Keratosis (0.63), Melanoma (0.58), and Dermatofibroma (0.54).
The authors conclude that this approach offers a promising method for dermatologists to improve the diagnosis of pigmented skin lesions.
The results provide a promising outlook for the further development of more accurate diagnostic models.
The developed web application is intended for research purposes only and has no clinical or diagnostic value.
The authors acknowledge that some randomized clinical trials are needed to find clinical evidence supporting the accuracy of these models.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 66.9% melanocytic nevi vs. 1.1% dermatofibroma), leading to biased performance where the model excels at majority classes (F1=0.93) but performs poorly on minority classes like dermatofibroma (F1=0.54) and melanoma (F1=0.58).

The developed web application is explicitly stated to have 'no clinical or diagnostic value' and is intended for research purposes only; the authors acknowledge that randomized clinical trials are needed to establish clinical evidence.

The dataset contains potential labeling errors, evidenced by the identification of identical images with conflicting labels in the top wrong predictions, suggesting data quality issues within the source dataset.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, relying solely on a test split from the HAM10000 dataset, which limits the generalizability of the 84.3% accuracy result.

The ground truth for the dataset is heterogeneous, with only 53.3% of lesions confirmed by histopathology, while others rely on follow-up (37.0%), expert consensus (9.0%), or in-vivo confocal microscopy (0.7%), introducing potential label noise.

The model was trained on a single hardware environment (Google Colab with NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU), and no information is provided regarding computational efficiency, inference time, or deployment constraints for clinical settings.
